Response: Georgia Institute of Technology, Graphics Lab and Humanoid Robotics Lab (originated 2011, created by C. Karen Liu and Mike Stilman); University of Washington Personal Robotics Lab; Open Source Robotics Foundation; Stanford University Movement Lab; Carnegie Mellon University

Response: Open-source C++ physics engine and kinematics/dynamics library for robotics and computer animation; provides kinematic and dynamic simulation of articulated structures with an emphasis on accurate and stable simulation for manipulation and locomotion research

column4: DART began at Georgia Tech in 2011 as a physics engine emphasizing stable simulation for locomotion. Used as a physics backend in Gazebo (alongside ODE and Bullet). Has an active main branch targeting DART 7 with modularization plans, and a release-6.x branch for stable fixes. Python bindings (dartpy) available via PyPI. Published in the Journal of Open Source Software (JOSS) 2018.

column4: Clean pip-based installation for Python users via dartpy. System package via apt also straightforward. Building from source uses standard CMake workflow. Installation documentation is clear and up to date. Comparable ease to MuJoCo and Drake pip installs.

column4: Modular architecture separating dynamics, collision detection, optimizer, and GUI components. Python bindings (dartpy) mirror C++ API. Supports URDF, SDF, and VSK skeleton formats. Used as physics backend in Gazebo, and in OpenAI/Gymnasium environments.
